era_context: The Ur III dynasty is in its mature phase, representing the last great Sumerian state. Egypt's First Intermediate Period continues, with regional fragmentation and weakened central authority. The Indus Valley Civilization remains at its urban peak. This period is characterized by the coexistence of several major civilizations with distinct political structures: the centralized bureaucratic state (Ur III), the decentralized regional kingdoms (Egypt), and the planned urban civilization with unknown political organization (Indus Valley).

events.title: Ur III temple economy operations
events.description: The Ur III state continued its sophisticated management of temple-based economic production. Thousands of clay tablets document the organization of labor gangs, textile workshops, and agricultural production. The temple of Enlil at Nippur and the temple of Nanna at Ur were major economic centers. This administrative system represents an early form of state-managed economy, though the relationship between temple and palace remains debated.

sources.name: Ur III administrative tablets from Nippur, Ur, and other sites

events.certainty_note: Tablets are dated by reign attribution. Absolute dating to -2217 is uncertain (±50–100 years).

events.title: Egyptian First Intermediate Period regional kingdoms
events.description: During the First Intermediate Period (c. 2181–2055 BCE in conventional chronology), Egypt was fragmented into competing regional kingdoms. No unified pharaonic authority existed. Local governors (nomarchs) ruled semi-independently, and the Nile Valley was divided into Upper and Lower Egyptian power centers. This period saw significant social and economic disruption following the Old Kingdom collapse. Specific events for -2217 are not attested.

sources.name: Egyptian administrative texts and tomb inscriptions from the First Intermediate Period
Historiographic consensus on First Intermediate Period dating

events.certainty_note: The First Intermediate Period is well-attested but poorly dated. Conventional chronology places it c. 2181–2055 BCE; alternative chronologies vary by ±100 years. Assignment to -2217 is speculative.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Egypt's First Intermediate Period is disputed. Some scholars argue for a shorter duration; others propose a longer period of fragmentation. The causes of the Old Kingdom collapse are debated: climate change (Nile failure), internal political breakdown, or external invasion. No single cause is confirmed.
historiographic_note: This year remains largely undocumented in narrative sources. Evidence comes from administrative tablets (Mesopotamia) and tomb inscriptions (Egypt). The Indus Valley Civilization is known only through archaeology. No contemporary accounts of political events, wars, or cultural developments survive outside administrative records. The historical record is fragmentary and heavily weighted toward economic and administrative data.

graph_edges.note: Part of the broader Ur III administrative system.
The First Intermediate Period ended with the rise of the Middle Kingdom c. 2055 BCE.
